pending.body: Extend `run resume` stale-owner recovery beyond `RunStatus::Running`. In `src/cli/run.rs:1983-2038`, the code only captures/reclaims an observed writer owner when the snapshot still says `running`, but the CLI does not actually close its `CliWriterLeaseGuard` until much later (`src/cli/run.rs:1950-1953` and `src/cli/run.rs:2130-2132`) after post-run sync. If the orchestrator dies after the engine has already written a resumable `failed`/`paused` snapshot but before `close()` runs, the orphaned `writer-*.lock` survives and the next `run resume` goes straight into `CliWriterLeaseGuard::acquire`, which fails with `ProjectWriterLockHeld`. Reclaim or ignore stale writer owners for resumable snapshots too, otherwise SIGTERM/OOM during post-run cleanup still wedges the run.

Do not treat `run.pid` records without `proc_start_ticks` as authoritative liveness. `FileSystem::is_pid_alive` in `src/adapters/fs.rs:329-347` returns true for any live PID when `proc_start_ticks` is `None`, `classify_running_snapshot_liveness` in `src/cli/run.rs:1531-1539` uses that to block `status`/`resume`, and the non-Linux stop path in `src/cli/run.rs:1705-1710` plus `src/cli/run.rs:1740-1753` will signal that PID directly. After PID reuse, a stale or legacy `run.pid` can therefore make an unrelated process look like the orchestrator, and on non-Linux `run stop` can send SIGTERM/SIGKILL to the wrong process. Treat missing `proc_start_ticks` as unsafe/stale everywhere, or add a platform-specific equivalent start-time check before trusting the PID.

status_summary: running: final review restart round 8 -> Plan and Implement
